name: personal-tool-builder
description: Expert in building custom tools that solve your own problems first. The best products often start as personal tools - scratch your own itch, build for yourself, then discover others have the same itch. Covers rapid prototyping, local-first apps, CLI tools, scripts that grow into products, and the art of dogfooding. Use when: build a tool, personal tool, scratch my itch, solve my problem, CLI tool.
capabilities: Guide you through identifying real problems worth solving with custom tools
Help you build CLI tools using Node.js or Python frameworks
Design local-first applications with SQLite or JSON file storage
Apply the 10-minute test to validate tool ideas before building
Structure the evolution from ugly script to shareable product
Teach dogfooding practices to improve tool usability
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The detected patterns (exec, backticks, file operations, SQLite) appear in code examples within documentation, not in executable skill code. This is an instructional skill that teaches tool-building methodologies without performing any actual system operations.
valueStatement: Stop waiting for the perfect tool to exist. Learn to build your own solutions from real pain points, starting with ugly scripts that work and evolving them into polished products only when proven useful.
userTitle: Build Custom Tools That Solve Your Problems
limitations: Does not provide actual tool execution - only guidance and code examples
Focuses on individual personal tools, not enterprise-scale solutions
Examples use JavaScript and Python - other languages not covered
Does not include cloud sync or multi-user collaboration patterns

useCases.targetUser: Developers tired of repetitive manual workflows
useCases.title: Automate Repetitive Tasks
useCases.description: Transform manual tasks you do 10x per day into automated scripts that save hours weekly.

useCases.targetUser: Knowledge workers with unique workflow requirements
useCases.title: Build Your Perfect Productivity App
useCases.description: Create a custom tool tailored to your exact workflow instead of adapting to existing software.

useCases.title: Turn Side Project Into Product
useCases.description: Evolve a personal script into a polished product that others want to use and pay for.

promptTemplates.title: Identify Your First Tool Idea
promptTemplates.prompt: I want to build a personal tool but I'm not sure what problem to solve. Help me identify 3 repetitive tasks I do regularly that could be automated. Ask me questions about my daily workflow to find the best opportunities.
promptTemplates.scenario: You have general interest in automation but no specific problem in mind

promptTemplates.title: Apply the 10-Minute Test
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm considering building a tool to [describe problem]. Help me apply the 10-minute test: validate that this is a real itch worth scratching, that I experience it weekly, and that I would actually use the solution daily.
promptTemplates.scenario: You have a potential tool idea and want to validate it before building

promptTemplates.title: Design a CLI Tool Architecture
promptTemplates.prompt: I want to build a CLI tool that [describe functionality]. Recommend the appropriate stack (Node.js or Python), suggest the command structure, and help me write the initial skeleton with proper argument parsing and error handling.
promptTemplates.scenario: You have a validated problem and are ready to start building

promptTemplates.title: Evolve Script to Product
promptTemplates.prompt: I have a working script that solves my problem but it's ugly and hardcoded. Help me refactor it into a maintainable tool with proper configuration, documentation, and error handling that could be shared with others.
promptTemplates.scenario: Your script works for you but you want to polish it for broader use

bestPractices: Start with the ugliest possible solution that works - perfection kills motivation
Use your tool daily to feel pain points and drive improvements naturally
Only add configuration, documentation, and error handling after the tool proves useful
antiPatterns: Building tools for imaginary users without real feedback from actual usage
Over-engineering with complex architecture before validating the core concept
Skipping dogfooding and missing obvious usability issues that daily use would reveal

faq.question: What if someone already built a tool for my problem?
faq.answer: Existing tools solve generic cases, not your exact workflow. Personal tools have perfect product-market fit for you. You can still use existing tools for reference, but custom tools often work better for specific needs.

faq.question: How do I know when to polish my ugly script?
faq.answer: After using it daily for 2-4 weeks, if you still reach for it automatically and feel annoyed when it breaks, it's proven useful. That's when investing in polish and documentation makes sense.

faq.question: Should I share my personal tools publicly?
faq.answer: Only after they've proven useful to you for at least a month. If others discover it and find value, great. But don't build for an audience - build for yourself first, share only if it feels natural.

faq.question: What programming language should I use?
faq.answer: Use whatever you're most comfortable with. Speed matters more than language choice. JavaScript and Python have good CLI libraries, but the best language is the one where you can build fastest.

faq.question: How do I handle data storage for personal tools?
faq.answer: Start with JSON files in your home directory. Upgrade to SQLite when you need queries or relationships. Avoid cloud storage unless sync is absolutely necessary - local-first means no server costs and works forever.

faq.question: What if I lose motivation to maintain my tool?
faq.answer: That's normal and okay. Personal tools don't need maintenance if they work. If it stops working after months of use, you either fix it because you need it, or you've outgrown the problem. Both are valid outcomes.
